Imtiaz Ahmed) Criminal Appeal No. 418 of 2018 (Khadim Ali and another Vs. The State and another) JUDGMENT. Date of hearing: 15.11.2021. Appellants by: Syed Zeeshan Haider, Advocate. State by: Ch. Asghar Ali Gill, Deputy Prosecutor General. Complainant by: Mr. Muhammad Aslam Khan Dhukkur, Advocate. SADIQ MAHMUD KHURRAM, J.- Khadim Ali son of Aman Ullah and Imtiaz Ahmad son of Aman Ullah (convicts) were tried along with Nazir Ahmad, Abdul Qadir alias Qadri and Muhammad Zakria alias Zakri ( the co- accused of the convicts, all since acquitted) by the learned Additional Sessions Judge, Sadiqabad in case FIR No. 256 of 2013 dated 22.07.2013 registered at Police Station Kot Sabzal District Rahim Yar Khan in respect of offences under sections 302, 34 and 109 PPC for committing the Qatl-i-Amd of Siraj Ahmad son of Raees Gaman Khan (deceased). The learned trial court vide judgment dated 29.05.2018, convicted Khadim Ali son of Aman M.R No. 23 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 418 of 2018 2 Ullah and Imtiaz Ahmad son of Aman Ullah (convicts) and sentenced them as infra: Khadim Ali son of Aman Ullah: Death under section 302(b) PPC as Tazir for committing Qatl-i-Amd of Siraj Ahmad son of Raees Gaman (deceased) and directed to pay Rs.100,000/- as compensation under section 544-A, Cr.P.C. to the legal heirs of the deceased namely Siraj Ahmad son of Raees Gaman and in case of default thereof, the convict was directed to further undergo six months of simple imprisonment.

The learned trial court vide judgment dated 29.05.2018, convicted Khadim Ali son of Aman M.R No. 23 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 418 of 2018 2 Ullah and Imtiaz Ahmad son of Aman Ullah (convicts) and sentenced them as infra: Khadim Ali son of Aman Ullah: Death under section 302(b) PPC as Tazir for committing Qatl-i-Amd of Siraj Ahmad son of Raees Gaman (deceased) and directed to pay Rs.100,000/- as compensation under section 544-A, Cr.P.C. to the legal heirs of the deceased namely Siraj Ahmad son of Raees Gaman and in case of default thereof, the convict was directed to further undergo six months of simple imprisonment. The convict was ordered to be hanged by his neck till dead. Imtiaz Ahmad son of Aman Ullah: Death under section 302(b) PPC as Tazir for committing Qatl-i-Amd of Siraj Ahmad son of Raees Gaman (deceased) and directed to pay Rs.100,000/- as compensation under section 544-A, Cr.P.C. to the legal heirs of the deceased namely Siraj Ahmad son of Raees Gaman and in case of default thereof, the convict was directed to further undergo six months of simple imprisonment. The convict was ordered to be hanged by his neck till dead. Nazir Ahmad, Abdul Qadir alias Qadri and Muhammad Zakria alias Zakri, the co-accused of the convicts, were acquitted by the learned trial court. 2. Feeling aggrieved, Khadim Ali son of Aman Ullah and Imtiaz Ahmad son of Aman Ullah (convicts) lodged Criminal Appeal No.418 of 2018 assailing their conviction and sentence. The learned trial court submitted Murder Reference No.23 of 2018 under section 374 Cr.P.C. seeking confirmation or otherwise of the sentences of death awarded to the appellants namely Khadim Ali son of Aman Ullah and Imtiaz Ahmad son of Aman Ullah. We intend to dispose of the Criminal Appeal No. 418 of 2018 and the Murder Reference No.23 of 2018 through this single judgment. M.R No. 23 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 418 of 2018 3 3. Precisely, the necessary facts of the prosecution case, as stated by Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4), the complainant of the case, are as under:- “Stated that on 22-7-2013 at about 6:30 P.M. I alongwith Zahoor Ahmad and Aman Ullah was going to the shop of deceased Siraj Ahmad which is situated in Tanki Bazar, Sanjarpur. When we reached Mohallah Mubarik Patyala near the house of Sofi Akhtar where Siraj Ahmad deceased was coming towards Bazar.

I alongwith Zahoor Ahmad and Aman Ullah was going to the shop of deceased Siraj Ahmad which is situated in Tanki Bazar, Sanjarpur. When we reached Mohallah Mubarik Patyala near the house of Sofi Akhtar where Siraj Ahmad deceased was coming towards Bazar. Suddenly two motorcycles accused namely Khadim, Imtiaz Qadri and Nazir came there. They pulled out their pistols and threatened deceased Siraj Ahmad of life threat. First fire was made by Khadim Ali which sustained on left side of Siraj Ahmad. Then second fire was made by Imtiaz Ahmad which Siraj Ahmad on his right side of chest. Abdul Qadir made fire which hit Siraj Ahmad trvrobrf (sic) on his left arm. Nazir Ahmad made a fire which Siraj Ahmad received on his right shoulder. Then Khadim made fire which was received by Siraj Ahmad on his right shoulder. Then Imtiaz Ahmad made fire which was received by Siraj Ahmad on the beneath of right ear. Another fire was made by Khadim Hussain which landed upon the right clavical of deceased. We tried to apprehend the accused persons they showed their weapons to us and threatened for dire consequences. We took Siraj Ahmad who succumbed to the injuries at the spot. Then we took Siraj Ahmad to RHC Sanjarpur. Police reached the hospital. Police inquired who is the legal heirs of the deceased, then I replied that I am brother of the deceased and I assured to the police that I was also present at the place of occurrence. Thanedar directed me to draft the application. Thanedar made the injury statement of deceased Siraj Ahmad. I presented the application ExP. D which was duly thumb marked by me. Thanedar handed over the dead body to Doctor for the purpose of conducting the autopsy. Thanedar asked me to accompany him and produce those witnesses who were present at the place of occurrence. I alongwith Thanedar reached the place of occurrence. The police officer collected 4 empties from the place of occurrence.

I alongwith Thanedar reached the place of occurrence. The police officer collected 4 empties from the place of occurrence. The Police officer sealed the four empties in a packet/jar. The I/O got the signatures/thumb impressions of PWs Zahoor and Aman Ullah. The 1.0 collected the blood stained earth from the place of death of deceased Siraj Ahmad and sealed the same and got the signatures of PWS Aman Ullah and Zahoor Ahmad on the paper. The 1.0 prepared rough site plan of the place of occurrence. Then we left for Hospital again with the 1.0. I received the dead body of the deceased Siraj Ahmad through receipt ExP.H from the police. On 25-7-2013, there was a Qul ceremony of my. deceased brother Siraj Ahmad at evening time. Bashir Ahmad and Khadim Hussain PWs came there and offered Fateha and told me about the occurrence. They told that on 22-7-2013 they were sitting at the Derah of Ch. Zakki at about 5:30/5:45 P.M. when Khadim, Imtiaz, Qadri and Nazir accused came there and told Ch. Zakki that they had come prepared and now order them what to do. Ch. Zakki ordered them to kill Mukhtiar Ahmad and Siraj Ahmad. Khadim and Bashir PWs told that they were still present at the Derah of Ch. Zakki when M.R No. 23 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 418 of 2018 4 at 06:45 P.M. in the evening, Khadim, Imtiaz, Qadari and Nazir came there again and told Ch. Zakki that they had killed Siraj Ahmad. Ch. Zakki told them that he ordered to kill both Siraj Ahmad and Mukhtiar Ahmad but you had not killed Mukhtiar, you go and kill Mukhtiar then I would produce you before the police. We submitted an application to the police but the police did not heed to it. Then we preferred to file application before the Justice of Peace. Justice of peace ordered the police to interrogate Ch. Zakki. Police associated Ch.

Zakki. Police associated Ch. Zakki in investigation regarding his abetting the accused. All the five accused present in the Court have killed my brother Siraj Ahmad on the instigation and abetment of Ch. Zakki. Accused be convicted and sentenced for the maximum.” 4. After the formal investigation of the case, the report under section 173 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 was submitted before the learned trial court, wherein the accused were sent to face trial. The learned trial court framed the charge against the accused on 01.09.2014, to which the accused pleaded not guilty and claimed trial. 5. The prosecution in order to prove its case, got statements of as many as thirteen witnesses recorded. The ocular account of the case was furnished by Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5). Abdul Sattar (PW-1) stated that on 22.07.2013 he identified the dead body of the deceased. Liaquat Ali, Patwari, (PW-3) stated that he prepared the scaled site plan of the place of occurrence (Exh.PE and Exh.PF).Bashir Ahmad (PW-6) and Khadim Hussain (PW-7) stated that on 22.07.2013 they saw the appellants arrive at the Dera of Muhammad Zakria alias Zaki (since acquitted) who ordered them to kill the deceased and saw the appellants return to the Dera of Muhammad Zakria alias Zaki (since acquitted) after murdering the deceased. Abdul Majeed, SI (PW-11) investigated the case on 22.07.2013 and detailed the facts of the investigation as conducted by him in his statement before the learned trial court. Muhammad Aslam, SI (PW-10) investigated the case from 15.08.2013 till 08.04.2014, arrested the appellants namely Imtiaz Ahmad and Khadim Ali on 13.12.2013 and detailed the facts of the investigation as conducted by him in his statement before the learned trial court. 6. The prosecution also got Dr.Wakeel Ahmad (PW-2) examined, who on 22.07.2013 was posted as Medical Officer at the RHC Sanjarpur and on the same day conducted the postmortem examination of the dead body of Siraj Ahmad son of Raees Gaman (deceased). A circular wound of entry 2 x 1 cm with burnt inverted margins on the supra pubic area of abdomen. 12) A circular wound of entry 1 x 1 cm with burnt inverted margins on the back of mid of right thigh and is directed towards the wound No.13. 13) A circular wound of exit 1 x 1 cm on the anterior aspect of mid of right thigh the margins of wound were everted and was directed towards wound no.12.................... REMARKS/OPINION: M.R No. 23 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 418 of 2018 7 In my opinion the cause of death was haemorrhage and shock due to injury No.1 to injury No.13. Injuries No. 3, 4, 5, 6 & 11 individually and injuries No. 1 to 13 collectively were sufficient to cause death an ordinary course of nature. All the injuries were ante-mortem and were caused by fire arm. Probable time that elapsed between the injuries and death was found immediate and between death and post mortem was within thirteen hours.” 7. On 27.01.2018, the learned Assistant District Public Prosecutor gave up the prosecution witnesses namely Zahoor Ahmad, Meer Hassan and Ali Gull as being unnecessary. On 17.02.2018, the learned Assistant District Public Prosecutor closed the prosecution evidence after tendering in evidence the report of the report of Punjab Forensic Science Agency, Lahore (Exh.PZZ) regarding the comparison of the empties (P-1/1-4) recovered from the place of occurrence and the pistol (P-3) recovered from the possession of the appellant namely Khadim Ali and the report of the Punjab Forensic Science Agency, Lahore (Exh.PZ) regarding the analysis of the blood stained earth. 8. After the closure of prosecution evidence, the learned trial court examined the appellants namely Khadim Ali son of Aman Ullah and Imtiaz Ahmad son of Aman Ullah under section 342 Cr.P.C. and in answer to question why this case against you and why the PWs have deposed against you, they replied that they had been involved in the case falsely and were innocent. The appellants namely Khadim Ali son of Aman Ullah and Imtiaz Ahmad son of Aman Ullah did not to get themselves examined under section 340 Cr.P.C however the appellant namely Khadim Ali submitted copy of FIR No. 218/2014 P.S Kotsabzal dated: 03-07-2014 u/s 457/380

On the conclusion of the trial, the learned Additional Sessions Judge, Sadiqabad, convicted and sentenced the appellants as referred to above. 10. The contention of the learned counsel for the appellants is that whole case is fabricated and false. The learned counsel for the appellants argued that the prosecution remained unable to prove the facts in issue and did not produce any unimpeachable, admissible and relevant evidence to prove the same. The learned counsel for the appellants further contended that the statements of Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) were not worthy of any reliance.

The learned counsel for the appellants argued that the prosecution remained unable to prove the facts in issue and did not produce any unimpeachable, admissible and relevant evidence to prove the same. The learned counsel for the appellants further contended that the statements of Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) were not worthy of any reliance. The learned counsel for the appellants also argued that the recoveries were full of procedural defects, of no legal worth and M.R No. 23 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 418 of 2018 9 value and result of fake proceedings. The learned counsel for the appellants finally submitted that the prosecution has totally failed to prove the case against the appellants beyond the shadow of doubt. 11. On the other hand, the learned Deputy Prosecutor General along with the learned counsel for the complainant contended that the prosecution has proved its case beyond shadow of doubt by producing independent witnesses. The learned Deputy Prosecutor General along with the learned counsel for the complainant further argued that the three deceased died as a result of injuries suffered at the hands of the appellants. The learned Deputy Prosecutor General alongwith the learned counsel for the complainant further contended that the medical evidence also corroborated the statements of Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5). The learned Deputy Prosecutor General alongwith the learned counsel for the complainant further argued that the recovery of the pistol (P-3) form the appellant namely Khadim Ali son of Aman Ullah and the recovery of the motorcycle (P-2) from the appellant namely Imtiaz Ahmad son of Aman Ullah also corroborated the ocular account. The learned Deputy Prosecutor General along with the learned counsel for the complainant further contended that there was no occasion for the prosecution witnesses, to substitute the real offenders with the innocent in this case. Lastly, the learned Deputy Prosecutor General along with the learned counsel for the complainant prayed for the rejection of appeal as lodged by the appellants namely Khadim Ali son of Aman Ullah and Imtiaz Ahmad son of Aman Ullah.

The learned Deputy Prosecutor General along with the learned counsel for the complainant further contended that there was no occasion for the prosecution witnesses, to substitute the real offenders with the innocent in this case. Lastly, the learned Deputy Prosecutor General along with the learned counsel for the complainant prayed for the rejection of appeal as lodged by the appellants namely Khadim Ali son of Aman Ullah and Imtiaz Ahmad son of Aman Ullah. M.R No. 23 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 418 of 2018 10 12. We have heard the learned counsels for the appellants, the learned counsel for the complainant, the learned Deputy Prosecutor General and with their assistance have perused the record and evidence recorded during the trial. 13. The whole prosecution case relies upon the statements of the prosecution witnesses namely Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) who gave the evidence with regard to the ocular account of the occurrence. It has also been brought on record that Siraj Ahmad (deceased) was the real brother of Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4). Though it has been admitted by Aman Ullah (PW-5) that he had friendship with the deceased as well as Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4), the complainant of the case, and he was on visiting terms with Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4), however, he was not related to them. Mere fact that a witness is not related to the deceased or the complainant of the case, is not the sole criteria to judge his independence or to accept or reject his testimony rather the true test is whether the evidence of a witness is credible and consistent with the circumstances of the case or otherwise. This is a well-known principle of appreciation of evidence that a witness to be relied upon, apart from being independent and disinterested, must be truthful and his evidence must be confidence inspiring and the mere fact that a witness is disinterested or having no motive to falsely implicate an accused in the case, can never be the sole reason believe his testimony. Reliance is placed on the case of “Lal Khan Vs. The State” (2006 SCMR 1846). Nonetheless during the course of cross-examination it was brought on record that Mumtaz, brother of the appellant namely Imtiaz Ahmad had

The State” (2006 SCMR 1846). Nonetheless during the course of cross-examination it was brought on record that Mumtaz, brother of the appellant namely Imtiaz Ahmad had M.R No. 23 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 418 of 2018 11 got lodged an F.I.R against Aman Ullah (PW-5) prior to the occurrence. Aman Ullah (PW-5) during cross-examination admitted as under:- “It is correct that Mumtaz, brother of accused Imtiaz, lodged a criminal case FIR No. 64/13 P.S. Kotsabzal against me............ It is correct that FIR No. 64/13 supra was registered on 22-2-2013 and 22-7-2013. It the instant FIR No.256/13 was registered on 22-7- 2013” Admittedly, the occurrence had taken place on a road laid within the area of Mohalla Mubarik Patyala, near the house of one Sufi Akhtar (not produced as a witness). Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) admitted during cross-examination that he was not the resident of the place of occurrence, rather the place of occurrence was at a distance of as many as one kilometer from his house. Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) during cross-examination admitted as under:- “Siraj Ahmad deceased was my brother. Aman Ullah PW is my friend............ Siraj Ahmad deceased and I resided in the same house. The house of Aman Ullah PW is about 8/9 Km from my house................... My house is at a distance of about 01-Km from the shop of deceased Siraj Ahmad. Mohallah Mubarik Patwalvi is 01-Km away from my house.” (emphasis supplied). Similarly, Aman Ullah (PW-5) also admitted that he was not resident of the place of occurrence rather his house was at a distance of as many as ten kilometres from the place of occurrence. Aman Ullah (PW-5) during cross- examination stated as under:- “My house is at a distance of about 8/9 Km from the house of Siraj deceased................ On the day of occurrence I did not go to the shop of the deceased On the day of occurrence, I had gone to the Bazar to purchase "Samosas" etc for "AFTARI" because its was the holy month "RAMZAAN". 1 reached the Bazar at 6:30 P.M.

Aman Ullah (PW-5) during cross- examination stated as under:- “My house is at a distance of about 8/9 Km from the house of Siraj deceased................ On the day of occurrence I did not go to the shop of the deceased On the day of occurrence, I had gone to the Bazar to purchase "Samosas" etc for "AFTARI" because its was the holy month "RAMZAAN". 1 reached the Bazar at 6:30 P.M. My house is at a distance of about 10-km from the said Bazar. On the day of occurrence, I did not go to the shop of PW Zahoor. From my house to the Bazar, I came by foot on that day for purchasing the M.R No. 23 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 418 of 2018 12 "Samosas" etc. It was July, 2013. It was summer.” (emphasis supplied). We have also perused the scaled site plans of the place of occurrence (Exh.PE and Exh.PF) as prepared by Liaquat Ali Patwari (PW-3) and the rough site plan of the place of occurrence (Exh.PY) as prepared by Abdul Majeed, SI (PW-11), the Investigating Officer of the case, and find that the residences of the witnesses namely Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) nor the places of work or any shops of the prosecution witnesses namely Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) are marked in the same. In view of the above mentioned facts, it can be validly held that the both the eye witnesses namely Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) “chance witnesses” and therefore were under a duty to explain and prove their presence at the place of occurrence, at the time of occurrence. We find that both the prosecution witnesses namely Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) failed to explain the circumstances in which they arrived at the place of occurrence and witnessed the same. We have noted with grave concern that no reason was mentioned by both the prosecution witnesses namely Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) in the written application (Exh.PG) and in the statements recorded under section 161 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 which necessitated them to travel to the place of occurrence.

We find that both the prosecution witnesses namely Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) failed to explain the circumstances in which they arrived at the place of occurrence and witnessed the same. We have noted with grave concern that no reason was mentioned by both the prosecution witnesses namely Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) in the written application (Exh.PG) and in the statements recorded under section 161 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 which necessitated them to travel to the place of occurrence. During the course of cross-examination it was claimed by Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) that he along with Zahoor Ahmad (given up PW) were going to the shop of Siraj Ahmad (deceased) situated in Tanki Bazar, Sanjarpur for the purpose of giving Siraj Ahmad (deceased) their clothes for sewing. It was also claimed by Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) that he and Zahoor Ahmad (given up pw) had purchased the M.R No. 23 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 418 of 2018 13 cloth from a shop owned by one Zulfiqar Punjabi at about 6.15 p.m. Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) during cross-examination stated as under:- “I told the police that I had gone to the shop of my deceased brother to give him cloths for stitching.................. I told the police that I had gone to the shop of my deceased brother to give him cloths for stitching.............. PW Aman Ullah met me at about 06:25 P.M, at "adda" Sanjarpur. PW Aman Ullah was alone at "Rehri". We remained with Aman Ullah at "Rehri" for about one and two minutes. I went to Aman Ullah by foot. Aman Ullah purchased "SAMOSAS" from said "Rehri". During the interregnum from 2:P.M. to 6:p.M. at the motorcycle shop of PW Zahoor neither my brother Siraj deceased nor I made any contact with each other. The shop of PW Zahoor was at a distance of about 200-yards from shop my brother Siraj deceased. Zahoor and I both purchased cloths from the shop at about 6:15 P.M.

The shop of PW Zahoor was at a distance of about 200-yards from shop my brother Siraj deceased. Zahoor and I both purchased cloths from the shop at about 6:15 P.M. We purchased the cloth from the shop of one Zulfiqar Punjabi.” (emphasis supplied) In this manner, a very specific claim was made by Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) that at the time of occurrence he as well as Zahoor Ahmad (given up pw) were carrying cloth for the purpose of its sewing into clothes by Siraj Ahmad (deceased) and therefore the said pieces of cloth should have been with the witness namely Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and he should have also showed the same to Abdul Majeed, SI (PW-11) at the time of his arrival at the place of occurrence,however, the said pieces of cloth in possession of the prosecution witness namely Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) were never produced before the Investigating Officer of the case on the day of occurrence or even subsequently during the investigation of the case.The said pieces of cloth which were to be given to Siraj Ahmad (deceased) for the purpose of sewing were also not observed to be present at the place of occurrence by Abdul Majeed, SI (PW-11), the Investigating Officer of the case, at the time of his arrival at the place of occurrence. The non- M.R No. 23 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 418 of 2018 14 production and the absence of any pieces of cloth as carried by Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) at the place of occurrence, at the time of arrival of the Investigating Officer of the case reveals that the prosecution failed to prove the reason for the arrival of Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) at the place of occurrence. Additionally, Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) also claimed that he had purchased the cloth which was to be tailored by Siraj Ahmad (deceased) from the shop of one Zulfiqar Punjabi, however obviously the said Zulfiqar Punjabi never joined the investigation of the case nor appeared before the learned trial court and remained a figment of imagination of Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4).

This omission strikes at the roots of the case of the prosecution and lays bare the untruthful and false claim of Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) to have been present at the place of occurrence, at the time of occurrence. Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) were under a duty to provide a convincing explanation, appealing to a prudent mind, of their presence at the crime spot otherwise their testimony has to be declared questionable and unacceptable. In this respect, reliance is placed on the case of “Muhammad Rafiq v. By improving their previous statements, Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) impeached their own credit. Article 151 of the Qanun-e- Shahadat Order 1984 provides as under: - M.R No. 23 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 418 of 2018 20 “151. Impeaching credit of witness. The credit of a witness may be impeached in the following ways by the adverse party, or, with the consent of the Court, by the party who calls him: by the evidence of persons who testify that they, from their knowledge of the witness, believe him to be un worthy of credit; by proof that the witness has been bribed, or has accepted the offer of a bribe, or has received any other corrupt inducement to give his evidence; by proof of former statements inconsistent with any part of his evidence which is liable to be contradicted;” As Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) introduced dishonest, blatant and substantial improvements to their previous statements and were duly confronted with their former statements, hence their credit stands impeached and Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) cannot be relied upon on being proved to have deposed with a slight, intended to mislead the court.

Impeaching credit of witness. The credit of a witness may be impeached in the following ways by the adverse party, or, with the consent of the Court, by the party who calls him: by the evidence of persons who testify that they, from their knowledge of the witness, believe him to be un worthy of credit; by proof that the witness has been bribed, or has accepted the offer of a bribe, or has received any other corrupt inducement to give his evidence; by proof of former statements inconsistent with any part of his evidence which is liable to be contradicted;” As Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) introduced dishonest, blatant and substantial improvements to their previous statements and were duly confronted with their former statements, hence their credit stands impeached and Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) cannot be relied upon on being proved to have deposed with a slight, intended to mislead the court. The august Supreme Court of Pakistan in the case of “Muhammad Ashraf Vs. State” (2012 SCMR 419) took a serious notice of the improvements introduced by witnesses and rejected their evidence. We, thus, are satisfied that the evidence of Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) has no intrinsic worth and is to be rejected out rightly. The august Supreme Court of Pakistan in a recent case reported as “Muhammad Mansha Vs. The State” (2018 SCMR 772) has enunciated the following principle: “Once the Court comes to the conclusion that the eye witnesses had made dishonest improvements in their statements then it is not safe to place reliance on their statements. It is also settled by this Court that whenever a witness made dishonest improvement in his version in order to bring his case in line with the medical evidence or in order to strengthen the prosecution case then his testimony is not worthy of credence”. M.R No. 23 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 418 of 2018 21 The august Supreme Court of Pakistan in the case reported as Muhammad Arif Vs. The State (2019 SCMR 631) has enunciated the following principle: “It is well established by now that when a witness improves his statement and moment it is observed that the said improvement was made dishonestly to strengthen the prosecution, such portion of his statement is to be discarded out of consideration. Having observed the improvements in the statements of both the witnesses of ocular account, we hold that it is not safe to rely on their testimony to maintain conviction and sentence of Muhammad Arif (appellant) on a capital charge.” 15. We have also noted that according to prosecution witnesses namely Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) they were in clear view, at a meagre distance, from the assailants and unarmed whereas the appellants were allegedly armed with firearm weapons whereas their co-accused were also armed with various weapons.

Having observed the improvements in the statements of both the witnesses of ocular account, we hold that it is not safe to rely on their testimony to maintain conviction and sentence of Muhammad Arif (appellant) on a capital charge.” 15. We have also noted that according to prosecution witnesses namely Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) they were in clear view, at a meagre distance, from the assailants and unarmed whereas the appellants were allegedly armed with firearm weapons whereas their co-accused were also armed with various weapons. Furthermore, it was the claim of Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) that as many as four accused in total fired from the pistols carried by them at the time of occurrence and in the midst of this indiscriminate firing, the witnesses namely Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5), did not receive even a single scratch on their persons during the whole occurrence. If Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) had been present in the view of the assailants, then they would not have been spared. Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) even did not claim that they did not receive any injury because they had hidden themselves or was not visible to the assailants. Blessing Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) with such an incredible consideration and showing them such favour is implausible and opposed to the natural behaviour of any accused. It is all the more illogical that being perceptive of the fact that if Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) were left alive, they would depose against the accused, even then the appellants and M.R No. 23 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 418 of 2018 22 their co-accused did not cause any injury to them. Neither there was any dearth of ammunition nor that of intent and opportunity on part of the appellants or their co-accused for not doing away with Mushtaq Ahmad (PW- 4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5). In this scenario, it is hard to believe that Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) would have been shown the courtesy of being not fired at all when they should also have been the target of the assailants. Such behaviour, on part of the accused, as deposed by the prosecution witnesses namely Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW- 5) runs counter to natural human conduct and behaviour.

In this scenario, it is hard to believe that Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) would have been shown the courtesy of being not fired at all when they should also have been the target of the assailants. Such behaviour, on part of the accused, as deposed by the prosecution witnesses namely Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW- 5) runs counter to natural human conduct and behaviour. Article 129 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984 allows the courts to presume the existence of any fact, which it thinks likely to have happened, regard being had to the common course of natural events and human conduct in relation to the facts of the particular case. We thus trust the existence of this fact, by virtue of the Article 129 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984, that the conduct of the assailants, as deposed to by Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW- 5), was opposed to the common course of natural events and human conduct. Hence, we are holding that Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4) and Aman Ullah (PW-5) were not present at the place of occurrence and did not witness the occurrence. 16. Thus, once there is suspicion regarding the death of a person, the following essential steps follow: firstly, there is a complete chain of police custody of the dead body, right from the moment it is taken into custody until it is handed over to the relatives, or in case they are unknown, then till his burial; secondly, post mortem examination of a dead person cannot be carried out without the authorization of competent police officer or the magistrate; thirdly, post mortem of a deceased person can only be carried out by a notified government Medical Officer; and finally, at the time of handing over the dead body by the police to the Medical Officer, all reports prepared by the investigating officer are also to be handed over to the said medical officer to assist his examination of the dead body. 11. It is usually the delay in the preparation of these police reports, which are required to be handed over to the medical officer along with the dead body, that result in the consequential delay of the post mortem examination of the dead person. To repel any adverse inference for such a delay, the prosecution has to provide justifiable reasons therefor, which in the present case is strikingly wanting.” 20. The question for determination before this Court now is that whether the evidence of the prosecution witnesses which has been disbelieved qua the acquitted co-accused of the appellants can be believed against the appellants. The proposition of law in Criminal Administration of Justice, that a common set of witnesses can be used for recording acquittal and conviction against the accused persons who were charged for the commission of same offence, is now a settled proposition. The august Supreme Court of Pakistan has recently held that partial truth cannot be allowed and perjury is a serious crime. This view stems from the notion that once a witness is found to have lied about a material aspect of a case, it cannot then be safely assumed that the said witness will declare the truth about any other aspect of the case. We have noted that the view should be that "the testimony of one detected in a lie was wholly worthless and must of necessity be rejected." If a witness is not coming out with the whole truth, then his evidence is liable to be discarded as a whole, meaning thereby that his evidence cannot be used either for convicting accused or acquitting some of them facing trial in the same case. This proposition is enshrined in the maxim falsus in uno falsus in omnibus. The august Supreme Court of Pakistan in Criminal Miscellaneous Application No. 200 of 2019 in Criminal Appeal No. 238-L of 2013 reported as PLD 2019 Supreme Court 527 has enunciated the following binding principles: - “The Pakistan Penal Code, 1860 (P.P.C.) contains many offences dealing with perjury and giving false testimony.

The very fact that there is a whole chapter, numbered XI, dedicated to such offences amply testifies to the fact that matters relating to giving of testimony were taken very seriously by those who drafted the P.P.C. and their continued retention in the P.P.C. ever since reflects the will of the M.R No. 23 of 2018 Crl. Holding that the rule falsus in uno, falsus in omnibus is inapplicable in this country practically encourages commission of perjury which is a serious offence in this country. A court of law cannot permit something which the law expressly forbids............................................................................................. 21. We may observe in the end that a judicial system which permits deliberate falsehood is doomed to fail and a society which tolerates it is destined to self-destruct. Truth is the foundation of justice and justice is the core and bedrock of a civilized society and, thus, any compromise on truth amounts to a compromise on a society's future as a just, fair and civilized society. Our judicial system has suffered a lot as a consequence of the above mentioned permissible deviation from the truth and it is about time that such a colossal wrong may be rectified in all earnestness.

The learned Deputy Prosecutor General and the learned counsel for the complainant have also relied upon the evidence of motive and submitted that it corroborated the ocular account. The motive of the occurrence as narrated by the prosecution witnesses namely Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4), Bashir Ahmad (PW-6) and Khadim Hussain (PW-7) was that Muhammad Zakria alias Zaki (since acquitted) wanted Siraj Ahmad dead. As mentioned above, Muhammad Zakria alias Zaki was tried by the learned trial court and acquitted. Neither the State nor Mushtaq Ahmad (PW-4), the complainant of the case, assailed the said acquittal of Muhammad Zakria alias Zaki, the co- accused of the appellants, who was alleged to had ordered the killing of Siraj Ahmad (deceased).It clearly proves that the prosecution witnesses failed to provide evidence enabling us to determine the truthfulness of the motive alleged. The unchallenged acquittal of Muhammad Zakria alias Zaki, the co- accused of the appellants has repercussions, entailing absolute and ardent collapse of the prosecution case with regard to the motive. The prosecution witnesses failed to prove the fact that the said motive was so compelling that it could have led the appellants to commit the Qatl-i-Amd of the deceased. There is a haunting silence with regard to the minutiae of motive alleged and what triggered the occurrence. No independent witness was produced by the prosecution to prove the motive as alleged.

The learned Deputy Prosecutor General and the learned counsel for the complainant have also laid much premium on the abscondence of the appellants namely Khadim Ali son of Aman Ullah and Imtiaz Ahmad son of Aman Ullah as proof of his guilt. The fact of abscondence of an accused can be used as a corroborative piece of evidence, which cannot be read in isolation but it has to be read along with substantive piece of evidence. The august Supreme Court of Pakistan has held in the case of “Asadullah v. Muhammad Ali” (PLD 1971 SC 541) that both corroborative and ocular evidence are to be read together and not in isolation. As regards abscondence, the august Supreme Court of Pakistan has held in the case of “Rasool Muhammad v. Asal Muhammad” (1995 SCMR 1373) that abscondence is only a suspicious circumstance. In the case of “Muhammad Sadiq v. Najeeb Ali” (1995 SCMR 1632) the august Supreme Court of Pakistan observed that abscondence itself has no value in the absence of any other evidence.

It is settled principle of law that for giving the benefit of the doubt it is not necessary that there should be so many circumstances rather if only a single circumstance creating reasonable doubt in the mind of a prudent person is available then such benefit is to be extended to an accused not as a matter of concession but as of right. The august Supreme Court of Pakistan in the case of “Muhammad Mansha Vs. The State” (2018 SCMR 772) has enunciated the following principle: “Needless to mention that while giving the benefit of doubt to an accused it is not necessary that there should be many circumstances creating doubt. If there is a circumstance which creates reasonable doubt in a prudent mind about the guilt of the accused, then the accused would be entitled to the benefit of such doubt, not as a matter of grace and concession, but as a matter of right. It is based on the maxim, "it is better that ten guilty persons be acquitted rather than one innocent person be convicted". Reliance in this behalf can be made upon the cases of Tariq Pervez v. The State (1995 SCMR 1345), Ghulam Qadir and 2 others v.The State (2008 SCMR 1221), Muqarab Khanv.The State (2009 SCMR 230) and Muhammad Zamanv.The State (2014 SCMR 749).” Reliance is also placed on the judgment of the august Supreme Court of Pakistan “Muqarab Khan Vs. the State” (2009 SCMR 230) in which it has been observed in paragraph No.13 of page 236 as infra: M.R No. 23 of 2018 Crl. Appeal No. 418 of 2018 45 “It is an axiomatic principle of law that in case of doubt, the benefit thereof must accrue in favour of the accused as matter of right and not of grace.
